questions.text: I do not naturally notice who is comfortable and who is uncomfortable in a group.
I routinely set aside time and money specifically for my own growth and enjoyment.
I enjoy crafting the strategy and assigning roles.
I feel that being of service is the core of my identity.
I am comfortable letting others make the final call.
I find that setting deadlines for myself, even arbitrary ones, keeps me productive.
I feel that my system has a natural resilience that brings me back to center quickly.
I pick up skills quickly through trial and error rather than structured practice.
I feel responsible for keeping a group connected and cohesive.
I get bored when a process becomes too routine and look for ways to shake things up.
I find that approaching human problems with structured analytical thinking is my most reliable method for producing outcomes that are both effective and just.
I find that I track whose opinions carry the most weight in a group even when no one has said so explicitly.
I find that prioritizing my own goals gives me the energy and clarity I need to thrive.
I find that caffeine, lack of sleep, or other stressors amplify my emotional reactivity dramatically.
I notice that I can hold space for difficult conversations without my own equilibrium being disrupted.
I find that I am not naturally tuned in to the emotional register of a conversation.
I feel that the pull to respond when I become aware of suffering is among the most trustworthy moral signals I experience.
I believe that the ability to engage with suffering on a purely intellectual level is an asset in most professional contexts.
